# Agency and structure in the linguistic landscape of the communities in Beijing, China: a discursive frame and geosemiotic analysis

[*Humanities and Social Sciences Communications*](/palcomms)
**volume 11**, Article number: 1702 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

2813 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

This article examines the linguistic landscapes (LL) of two communities in Beijing, China, to investigate the agency and structure embodied in them. A geosemiotic and discursive approach is employed to the collected data based on geosemiotics and discursive frame. It reveals the LL in the surveyed communities in Beijing can be mainly divided into three discursive frames: the civic frame, the promotional frame, and the livelihood frame, each with its own exclusive geosemiotic characteristics of agency and structure. A separate examination of each frame reveals the specific representations of and interactions between agency and structural factors within each one. Furthermore, a longitudinal analysis of these three frames indicates the existence of a concrete dynamic equilibrium relationship between agency and structure. It can be summarized that one of the existing landscape construction modes in the communities in Beijing is characterized by the balanced agency-structure dynamics, which can serve as a reference for LL construction and research in other regions.

## Introduction

The linguistic landscape is widely acknowledged as an essential part of the modern cityscape (Shohamy et al. [2010](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR82 "Shohamy E, Ben-Rafael E, Barni E (2010) Linguistic landscape in the city. Multilingual Matters, Bristol")). Its emergence as a sub-field of linguistics has brought renewed attention to linguistic signs and language within the urban landscape (Rasinger, [2018](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR77 "Rasinger SM (2018) Constructing Banglatown: linguistic landscapes in London’s east end. Linguistic Landsc. 4(1):72–95")). With more attention being paid to commercial or urban centers in cities (Qi and Li, [2022](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR75 "Qi W, Li HE (2022) Mapping the linguistic landscape of historic tourist attractions: a case study of South Luogu Alley in Beijing. J Landsc Res 14(2):5"); Wang, [2013](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR92 "Wang J (2013) Linguistic landscape of China: a case study of shop signs in Beijing. Stud Lit Lang 6(1):40–47"); Xiao, [2018](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR97 "Xiao R (2018) The linguistic landscape of Beijing tourism spots: a field-based sociolinguistic approach. Int J Lang Lit Linguist 4(1):23–28"); Xiao and Lee, [2022](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR98 "Xiao R, Lee C (2022) English in the linguistic landscape of the Palace Museum: a field-based sociolinguistic approach. Soc. Semiotics 32(1):95–114"); Xv, [2020](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR99 "Xv XL (2020) The markedness theory for linguistic landscape research: a case study of Beijing 798 Art Zone. Chin J Lang Policy Plan 5(4):74-82+12")), in recent work in the LL field, little focus, however, has been on the basic unit of urban governance system — the communities[Footnote 1](#Fn1). Compared to the commercial or urban centers, the communities could represent the real life of all aspects, the actual macro governance, and the interaction among macro, meso and micro actors. Nevertheless, the dynamics of multi-level agencies and structure embodied in the LL has rarely been investigated, especially those in communities (Buchstaller, [2021](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR17 "Buchstaller I (2021) Community cityscape: modes of engagement and co-construction of the streetscape. Linguist Vanguard 7(s5):218–239"); Lado, [2011](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR55 "Lado B (2011) Linguistic landscape as a reflection of the linguistic and ideological conflict in the Valencian community. Int J Multiling 8(2):135–150"); Nambu, [2021](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR69 "Nambu S (2021) Linguistic landscape of immigrants in Japan: a case study of Japanese Brazilian communities. J Multiling Multicult Dev 45:1–17"); Nana, [2020](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR70 "Nana T (2020) The linguistic landscape of Korean town community in Yiwu. 인문사회 11(1):495–506"); Strange, [2022](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR87 "Strange L (2022) The discursive construction of gender and agency in the linguistic landscape of Ireland’s 2018 abortion referendum campaign. Crit Discourse Stud. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/17405904.2022.2150669
                  
                "); Yao, [2023](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR101 "Yao XF (2023) Commodification or shared ownership? a case study of Chinese communities in the linguistic landscape of Bendigo. Appl Linguist Rev 14(3):447–472")).

The term “community” has its etymological roots in Latin, signifying a state of intimacy and something in common.[Footnote 2](#Fn2) Ferdinand Tönnies, the 19th-century German sociologist, was the first to propose “community” (Gemeinschaft) in the sociological field (Tönnies, [1887](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR90 "Tönnies F (1887) Gemeinschaft und gesellschaft: abhandlung des communismus und des socialismus als empirischer culturformen. Fues’s Verlag, Leipzig"); Tönnies and Loomis, [2017](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR91 "Tönnies F, Loomis CP (2017) Community and society. Routledge, New York")). The English term “community” was translated into Chinese as “社区” in the 1930s, marking its formal introduction into China (Fei, [2009](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR27 "Fei XT (2009) The sixth volume of the collected works of Fei Xiaotong (1948–1949). Inner Mongolia People’s Publishing House, Inner Mongolia"); Wang, [2002](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR94 "Wang XZ (2002) What is community and its significance. Zhejiang Acad J 2:20–24")). Influenced by the humanistic ecology of the Chicago School (Hoffer, [1931](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR41 "Hoffer CR (1931) Understanding the community. Am J Sociol 36(4):616–624"); Park, [1936](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR72 "Park RZ (1936) Human ecology. Am. J. Sociol. 42(1):1–15")), it was initially defined in China as a social unit constituted by people living in a common geographical area, highlighting its territorial nature (Fei, [2009](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR27 "Fei XT (2009) The sixth volume of the collected works of Fei Xiaotong (1948–1949). Inner Mongolia People’s Publishing House, Inner Mongolia"); Wang, [2002](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR94 "Wang XZ (2002) What is community and its significance. Zhejiang Acad J 2:20–24")). By the late 20th century, China initiated a community-building campaign aimed at reforming communities into the state governance unit (Yang, [2007](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR100 "Yang M (2007) Community as state governance unit: a case study on residents community participation and cognition in the process of community building campaign. Sociol Stud 4:137-164+245")). According to the Modern Chinese Dictionary (Dictionary Editing Office of the Institute of Linguistics, Chinese Academy of Social Sciences, [2016](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR21 "Dictionary Editing Office of the Institute of Linguistics, Chinese Academy of Social Sciences (ed) (2016) Modern Chinese dictionary, 7th edn. Commercial Press, Beijing")), “community” in the Chinese context currently encompasses two primary interpretations. The first pertains to a social unit within a specific geographical area, such as the Chinese community in San Francisco. The second refers to a subdivided residential area within cities and towns in China based on geographic location, with each community representing one of the smaller governance units under a subdistrict within cities. In this paper, the term “community” is used to refer to the second definition. To illustrate, every city in China comprises numerous subdivided communities, such as the Wanshousi community, Changwa community, and many other communities in Beijing.

As the capital and a metropolis in China, Beijing plays an essential role in politics, economy, and culture, and is a typical city with Chinese characteristics. Communities in Beijing are filled with LL on various themes closely related to the people’s livelihood. These themes form an integral part of urban LL. They exert both informational and symbolic functions for information-conveying and ideology-establishment (Landry and Bourhis, [1997](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR58 "Landry R, Bourhis RY (1997) Linguistic landscape and ethnolinguistic vitality. J Lang Soc Psychol 16(1):23–49")). Particularly within highly socialized contexts, under the macro governance and meso or micro implementation, multi-layer actors closely interweave together for the public structure. Thus, drawing on the discursive frame (Coupland and Garrett, [2010](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR20 "Coupland N, Garrett P (2010) Linguistic landscapes, discursive frames and metacultural performance: the case of Welsh Patagonia. Int J Sociol Lang. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1515/ijsl.2010.037
                  
                "); Goffman, [1974](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR32 "Goffman E (1974) Frame analysis: an essay on the organization of experience. Harvard University Press, Cambridge"); Kallen, [2010](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR50 "Kallen JL (2010) Changing landscapes: language, space and policy in the Dublin linguistic landscape. In: Jaworski A, Thurlow C (eds) Semiotic landscapes: language, image, space. Continuum, London, p 41–58")) and geosemiotics (Scollon and Scollon, [2003](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR81 "Scollon R, Scollon SW (2003) Discourse in place: language in the material world. Routledge, New York")), this article aims to delve into the LL of the communities in Beijing, China, through case studies of two of these communities, and uncover the dynamics of multi-layer actors’ agencies and structural forces involved. This enables us to summarize one of the existing modes of landscape construction in Beijing from the perspective of agency and structure.

This article is structured as follows: section “Literature review” presents the literature review on the LL field; the theoretical framework and research method are introduced in section “Theoretical framework” and “Method”, respectively; Section “Findings and discussion” presents a systematic analysis of the collected data and displays the empirical findings, followed by the conclusion in Section “Conclusion”.

## Literature review

### Linguistic landscape

The linguistic landscape is thoroughly defined by Landry and Bourhis ([1997](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR58 "Landry R, Bourhis RY (1997) Linguistic landscape and ethnolinguistic vitality. J Lang Soc Psychol 16(1):23–49")), who argue that “the language of public road signs, advertising billboards, street names, place names, commercial shop signs, and public signs on government buildings combines to form the linguistic landscape of a given territory, or urban agglomeration” (p. 25). Gorter and Cenoz ([2023](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR36 "Gorter D, Cenoz J (2023) A panorama of linguistic landscape studies. Channel View Publications, Bristol")) posit that the roots of the LL field were sown in the 1970s, but significant progress was not made until the 1990s before it established itself as a proper field in the mid-2000s. Up to now, the LL field has gone through three waves (Bolton et al. [2020](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR14 "Bolton K, Botha W, Lee SL (2020) Linguistic landscapes and language contact. In: Hickey R (ed) The handbook of language contact, 2nd edn. John Wiley and Sons, Malden, p 281-300"); Gorter and Cenoz, [2023](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR36 "Gorter D, Cenoz J (2023) A panorama of linguistic landscape studies. Channel View Publications, Bristol")). The studies in the first wave put the emphasis on the quantitative distributive analysis of languages on public signs (Amos, [2016](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR2 "Amos HW (2016) Chinatown by numbers: defining an ethnic space by empirical linguistic landscape. Linguistic Landsc 2(2):127–156"); Amos and Soukup, [2020](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR3 "Amos HW, Soukup B (2020) Quantitative 2.0: towards variationist linguistic landscape study (VaLLS) and a standard canon of LL variables. In: Malinowski D, Tufi S (eds) Reterritorializing linguistic landscapes: questioning boundaries and opening spaces. Bloomsbury, London, pp 56–66"); Backhaus, [2007](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR6 "Backhaus P (2007) Linguistic landscapes: a comparative study of urban multilingualism in Tokyo. Multilingual Matters, Clevedon"); Banda and Jimaima, [2017](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR7 "Banda F, Jimaima H (2017) Linguistic landscapes and the sociolinguistics of language vitality in multilingual contexts of Zambia. Multilingua 36(5):595–625"); Gorter, [2006a](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR34 "Gorter D (2006a) Further possibilities for linguistic landscape research. In: Gorter D (ed) Linguistic landscape: a new approach to multilingualism. Multilingual Matters, Clevedon, p 81–89"); Gorter et al. [2012](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR37 "Gorter D, Marten HF, Van Mensel L (2012) Minority languages in the linguistic landscape. Palgrave Macmillan, Basingstoke"); Kerry, [2017](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR51 "Kerry VJ(2017) The construction of hegemonic masculinity in the semiotic landscape of a crossfit ‘cave’. Vis Commun. 16(2):209–237"); Lamarre, [2014](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR57 "Lamarre P (2014) Bilingual winks and bilingual wordplay in Montreal’s linguistic landscape. Int J Sociol Lang. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1515/ijsl-2014-0008
                  
                "); Lanza and Woldemariam, [2014](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR59 "Lanza E, Woldemariam H (2014) Multilingualism and local literacy practices in Ethiopia: language contact in regulated and unregulated spaces. Multiling. Margins 1(1):55–74"); Shohamy et al. [2010](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR82 "Shohamy E, Ben-Rafael E, Barni E (2010) Linguistic landscape in the city. Multilingual Matters, Bristol"); Shohamy and Gorter, [2009](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR83 "Shohamy E, Gorter D (2009) Linguistic landscape: expanding the scenery. Routledge, New York"); Woldemariam and Lanza, [2015](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR96 "Woldemariam H, Lanza E (2015) Imagined community: the linguistic landscape in a diaspora. Linguistic Landsc. 1(1/2):172–190")). The second wave experiences the “qualitative or ethnographic turn”, such as Calvet’s ([1990](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR18 "Calvet LJ (1990) Des mots sur les murs. une comparaison entre paris et dakar. In: Chaudenson R (ed) Des langues et des villes (Actes du colloque international à dakar, du 15 au 17 Décembre 1990). Didier Érudition, Paris, p 73-83")) comparative analysis, Spolsky and Cooper’s ([1991](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR86 "Spolsky B, Cooper RL (1991) The languages of Jerusalem. Oxford, Clarendon")) historical analysis, Scollon and Scollon’s ([2003](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR81 "Scollon R, Scollon SW (2003) Discourse in place: language in the material world. Routledge, New York")) geosemiotics, Kallen’s ([2010](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR50 "Kallen JL (2010) Changing landscapes: language, space and policy in the Dublin linguistic landscape. In: Jaworski A, Thurlow C (eds) Semiotic landscapes: language, image, space. Continuum, London, p 41–58")) frame analysis, and many other studies (Blackwood and Tufi, [2015](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR11 "Blackwood RJ, Tufi S (2015) The linguistic landscape of the mediterranean: French and Italian coastal cities. Palgrave Macmillan, Basingstoke"); Blommaert, [2013](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR13 "Blommaert J (2013) Ethnography, superdiversity and linguistic landscapes: chronicles of complexity. Multilingual Matters, Bristol"); Jaworski and Thurlow, [2010](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR48 "Jaworski A, Thurlow C (2010) Semiotic landscapes: language, image, space. Continuum, London"); Pennycook and Otsuji, [2015](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR73 "Pennycook A, Otsuji E (2015) Making scents of the landscape. Linguistic Landsc 1(3):191–212")). And the third critical wave is closely linked to political and social conflict, protest, identity, and social justice (Blackwood et al. [2016](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR12 "Blackwood R, Lanza E, Woldemariam H (eds) (2016) Negotiating and contesting identities in linguistic landscapes. Bloomsbury, London"); Bolton et al. [2020](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR14 "Bolton K, Botha W, Lee SL (2020) Linguistic landscapes and language contact. In: Hickey R (ed) The handbook of language contact, 2nd edn. John Wiley and Sons, Malden, p 281-300")).

Linguistic landscapes form an integral part of the modern cityscape, and its emergence as a field of study in linguistics has redirected attention to linguistic signs and language within the urban landscape (Rasinger, [2018](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR77 "Rasinger SM (2018) Constructing Banglatown: linguistic landscapes in London’s east end. Linguistic Landsc. 4(1):72–95"): 74). Over the past decades, there has been a surge in studies investigating the linguistic landscapes prevalent in major urban centers (ibid, p. 74; Shohamy et al. [2010](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR82 "Shohamy E, Ben-Rafael E, Barni E (2010) Linguistic landscape in the city. Multilingual Matters, Bristol")), such as Jerusalem (Ben-Rafael et al. [2006](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR9 "Ben-Rafael E, Shohamy E, Hasan Amara M, Trumper-Hecht N (2006) Linguistic landscape as symbolic construction of the public space: the case of Israel. Int J Multiling 3(1):7–30"); Nadel and Fishman, [1977](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR68 "Nadel E, Fishman JA (1977) English in Israel: a sociolinguistic study. In: Fishman JA, Cooper RL, Conrad AW (eds) The spread of English: the sociology of English as an additional language. Newbury House, Rowley, p 137–167"); Rosenbaum et al. [1977](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR78 "Rosenbaum Y, Nadel E, Cooper RL, Fishman JA (1977) English on Keren Kayemet street. In: Fishman JA, Cooper RL, Conrad AW (eds) The spread of English: the sociology of English as an additional language. Newbury House, Rowley, p 179–196"); Spolsky and Cooper, [1983](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR85 "Spolsky B, Cooper RL (1983) The languages of jerusalem; Arab-Jewish relation in the old city. Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan"), [1991](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR86 "Spolsky B, Cooper RL (1991) The languages of Jerusalem. Oxford, Clarendon")), Tokyo (Backhaus, [2006](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR5 "Backhaus P (2006) Multilingualism in Tokyo: a look into the linguistic landscape. Int J Multiling 3(1):52–66"), [2007](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR6 "Backhaus P (2007) Linguistic landscapes: a comparative study of urban multilingualism in Tokyo. Multilingual Matters, Clevedon")), Bangkok (Huebner, [2006](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR42 "Huebner T (2006) Bangkok’s linguistic landscapes: environmental print, codemixing and language change. Int J Multiling 3(1):31–51")), Washington, DC (Lou, [2010](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR62 "Lou JJ (2010) Chinese on the side: the marginalization of Chinese in the linguistic and social landscapes of Chinatown in Washington, DC. In: Shohamy E, Ben-Rafael E, Barni M (eds) Linguistic landscape in the city. Multilingual Matters, Bristol, p 96–114")), Antwerp (Blommaert, [2013](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR13 "Blommaert J (2013) Ethnography, superdiversity and linguistic landscapes: chronicles of complexity. Multilingual Matters, Bristol")), Beijing (Dong, [2020](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR23 "Dong J (2020) Space, signs, and legitimate workers’ identities: an ethnography of a Beijing “urban village. Soc Semiotics 30(2):151–167"), [2021](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR24 "Dong J (2021) Labor is the most glorious’: chronotopic linguistic landscaping and the making of working class identities. Lang Commun 80:1–10"); Wang, [2013](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR92 "Wang J (2013) Linguistic landscape of China: a case study of shop signs in Beijing. Stud Lit Lang 6(1):40–47"); Xiao, [2018](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR97 "Xiao R (2018) The linguistic landscape of Beijing tourism spots: a field-based sociolinguistic approach. Int J Lang Lit Linguist 4(1):23–28"); Xiao and Lee, [2022](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR98 "Xiao R, Lee C (2022) English in the linguistic landscape of the Palace Museum: a field-based sociolinguistic approach. Soc. Semiotics 32(1):95–114")), Hong Kong (Guinto, [2019](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR38 "Guinto N (2019) The place/s of Tagalog in Hong Kong’s central district. Linguistic Landsc. 5(2):160–178"); Jaworski and Lou, [2020](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR47 "Jaworski A, Lou JJ (2020) #Wordswewear: mobile texts, expressive persons, and conviviality in urban spaces. Soc. Semiotics 31(1):108–135"); Lai, [2013](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR56 "Lai ML (2013) The linguistic landscape of Hong Kong after the change of sovereignty. Int J Multiling 10(3):251–272")), Paris (Zhao, [2021](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR103 "Zhao FZ (2021) Linguistic landscapes as discursive frame: chinatown in Paris in the eyes of new Chinese migrants. Linguistic Landsc. 7(2):235–257")), London (Jaworski and Lou, [2020](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR47 "Jaworski A, Lou JJ (2020) #Wordswewear: mobile texts, expressive persons, and conviviality in urban spaces. Soc. Semiotics 31(1):108–135")). Due to the almost exclusive focus on the language of cities, some scholars call LL “linguistic cityspace” instead of “linguistic landscape” (Coulmas, [2009](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR19 "Coulmas F (2009) Linguistic landscaping and the seed of the public sphere. In: Shohamy E, Gorter D (eds) Linguistic landscape: expanding the scenery. Routledge, New York, p 13–24"); Gorter, [2006b](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR35 "Gorter D (2006b) Linguistic landscape: a new approach to multilingualism. Multilingual Matters, Clevedon"); Spolsky, [2009](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR84 "Spolsky B (2009) Prolegomena to a sociolinguistic theory of public signage. In: Shohamy E, Gorter D (eds) Linguistic landscape: expanding the scenery. Routledge, New York, p 25–39")).

In recent times, there is also a growing interest among scholars in China in the LL field, with a specific focus on major cities (Gorter and Cenoz, [2023](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR36 "Gorter D, Cenoz J (2023) A panorama of linguistic landscape studies. Channel View Publications, Bristol")), such as Beijing (Dong, [2020](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR23 "Dong J (2020) Space, signs, and legitimate workers’ identities: an ethnography of a Beijing “urban village. Soc Semiotics 30(2):151–167"), [2021](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR24 "Dong J (2021) Labor is the most glorious’: chronotopic linguistic landscaping and the making of working class identities. Lang Commun 80:1–10"); Wang, [2013](/articles/s41599-024-04236-2#ref-CR92 "Wang J (2013) Linguistic landscape of China: a case study of shop signs in Beijing. Nonetheless, there remains little attention paid to communities in Beijing, which is regarded as the basic unit of the urban governance system and plays a key role in urban and social construction.

### Agency and str

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 5: a deep learning–based OCR analysis of street view images ---
URL: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44212-025-00071-1

SUMMARY:
This study examines Seoul’s linguistic landscapes using a novel framework that integrates large-scale street view image (SVI) datasets, an enhanced deep learning–based optical character recognition (OCR) model, and geospatial analytics. We propose a five-step method within this framework: data collection, image processing, application of a multilingual OCR detection algorithm, identification of linguistic landscapes, and verification of results. By integrating text detection and language recognition with refined dictionaries, the proposed model offers a systematic method for identifying and analyzing multilingual signs in urban environments, ensuring greater precision in linguistic landscape research. In this context, the present study contributes to the understanding of linguistic landscapes using SVI big data and an OCR model to empirically analyze Seoul and by proposing a globally applicable systematic analytical framework.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Analysis of 3437 signs revealed English (61.65%) and Spanish (24.16%) as dominant languages, indicating cultural and linguistic hierarchies within urban environments.
Multilingual signs were infrequently observed, particularly in neighborhoods with Bilingual Programs, suggesting limited representation of diverse linguistic communities in public spaces.
The linguistic landscape dynamics correlate with socio-economic conditions; affluent areas displayed richer multilingual signage, reflecting the community's cultural diversity and economic vitality.
